rulings.Case Title: CARA L DROGUS VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Granting Petition For Writ Of Mandate
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 4, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONER CARA DROGUS'S Motion For Order Granting Petition For Writ Of Mandate Petitioner Cara L. Drogus's motion for an order granting verified petition for writ of mandate is granted for lack of jurisdiction. It is undisputed that the incident that gave rise to the administrative decision took place at the East Beach Parking Lot in Crissy Field in the Presidio of San Francisco. The Presidio is a federal enclave in which, with limited exceptions not applicable here, the federal government has exclusive jurisdiction. (E.g., Standard Oil Co. of California v. California (1934) 291 U.S. 242, 243-244; United States v. Watkins (N.D. Cal. 1927) 22 F.2d 437, 438-441; see also Volk v. United States (N.D. Cal. 1999) 57 F.Supp.2d 888, 893 n.2 ["Aside from being widely recognized within the Northern District of California, this undisputed fact [that the Presidio is under federal jurisdiction] has been formally confirmed both legislatively and judicially."]; U.S. v. California Department of Transportation (N.D. Cal. 2009) 693 F.Supp.2d 1082, 1084 [noting that in 1994, jurisdiction over the Presidio was transferred from the United States Army to the United States National Park Service].) The City and County of San Francisco Department of Animal Care and Control therefore lacked jurisdiction to issue the decision and order challenged by the petition. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the petitioner is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ENTREPRENEUR MEDIA INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. SCOTT SMITH et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Denying Motion For Sanctions Pursuant To C.C.P. 128.5 And 128.7
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 4, 2019, Line 15, CROSS COMPLAINANT SCOTT SMITH'S Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Denying Motion For Sanctions Pursuant To C.C.P. 128.5 And 128.7 Defendant and Cross-Complainant Scott Smith's motion for reconsideration of the Court's March 15, 2019 order denying Defendant's motion for sanctions under Code of Civil Procedure sections 128.5 and 128.7 is denied, and the Court declines Mr. Smith's invitation to reconsider the order on its own motion pursuant to Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal.4th 1094. None of the purported new evidence Mr. Smith seeks to present affects the basis for the Court's original ruling, i.e., that a self-represented litigant may not recover an award of attorneys' fees or other expenses as sanctions. (Musaelian v. Adams (2009) 45 Cal.4th 512, 517, 520.) The Court acknowledges that Code of Civil Procedure section 128.5(f) and 128.7(d) provide that sanctions awarded under those sections may consist of, or include, directives of a nonmonetary nature or an order to pay a penalty into court. However, Mr. Smith did not seek the imposition of any non-monetary sanctions. In any event, the Court has reviewed Mr. Smith's voluminous declaration and exhibits, and finds that Mr. Smith has not met his burden to show that EMI and its counsel have engaged in conduct warranting the imposition of sanctions, whether payable to Mr. Smith or to the Court. The fact that Mr. Jurin filed a declaration adverse to Mr. Smith in an adversary proceeding in bankruptcy court in 2006, or that he was once seen "in the company of EMI's counsel David J. Cook" (Smith Decl. 11, 14 & Ex. I), does not establish, as Mr. Smith alleges, that EMI and Mr. Cook "have for years colluded with" Mr. Jurin, engaged in "abusive tactics" against Mr. Smith, or committed perjury, nor does Mr. Cook's silence in the face of Mr. Smith's accusations constitute an admission of such collusion or other improper conduct. The Court denies Mr. Smith's charge that it is, or appears to be, biased against pro se litigants, persons of color, women, and defendants, or that it has accorded disparate treatment to Mr. Smith and his opponent. The Court observes that both parties are locked in acrimonious, burdensome, and unproductive litigation, replete with personal accusations and counter-accusations of lying, fraud, and contempt. This litigation was filed on April 18, 2013, is reflected in over 1,200 entries on the Court's docket, has required dozens of court hearings and case management conferences, and resulted nearly 18 months ago in entry of a final judgment on the underlying fraudulent conveyance claim, as well as multiple petitions for writs of mandate and at least one pending appeal. It has imposed extraordinary burdens on the Court. The parties are strongly admonished to refrain from further invective and ad hominem attacks, whether directed at each other or at the Court, and to limit their filings to those necessary to reach a final resolution of the merits of any remaining factual or legal issues. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: R & G EXECUTIVE LOUNGE, INC. VS. HENRY HUNG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For An Order Sealing The November 13 2017 Declaration Of Monica Ip And B A Protecive Order Limiting The Parties Us Of The November 13 2017 Declaration Of Monica Ip To This Action
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 4, 2019, Line 2, CROSS DEFENDANT R & G EXECUTIVE LOUNGE, INC'S Motion For An Order Sealing The November 13 2017 Declaration Of Monica Ip And B A Protecive Order Limiting The Parties Us Of The November 13 2017 Declaration Of Monica Ip To This Action Defendant R & G Executive Lounge, Inc.'s motion for entry of an order sealing the November 13, 2017 declaration of Monica Ip is denied. Substantially all of the compensation and related financial information contained in that declaration was disclosed in the April 20, 2018 declaration of Ms. Ip, which was publicly filed on that date in unredacted form by Defendants and Cross-Complainants Henry Hung and Joe K. Ling in support of their ex parte application for a temporary restraining order and order to show cause re preliminary injunction. R & G's motion for entry of a protective order limiting the use of the Ip declaration to this action is denied. The declaration does not constitute a Company document produced in discovery in this action, and in any event it has already been provided to Mr. Wong's experts in another matter. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: CARLETTE SMITH VS. FCA US, LLC
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Attorney Fees, Costs And Expenses
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 4, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF CARLETTE SMITH'S Motion For Attorney Fees, Costs And Expenses Plaintiff Carlette Smith's motion for attorney fees, costs, and expenses is granted in the amount of $8,242.00 in attorneys' fees and $1,472.05 in costs and expenses. In Defendants' January 18, 2018 Code of Civil Procedure section 998 offer, which Plaintiff accepted, Defendants offered "to pay reasonable costs, expenses and attorney's fees based on actual time expended pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure Section 1794(b) as stipulated by the parties or, if the parties cannot agree, upon motion to the court having jurisdiction over this action." Despite the emphasized language, Plaintiff never made any effort to stipulate to an award of reasonable attorneys' fees, costs and expenses. Further, Plaintiff's more than one-year delay in addressing the fee issues required its counsel to travel to San Francisco to attend two order to show cause hearings and file this motion, all of which could have been avoided, had Plaintiff timely sought to resolve its fee request. Under the circumstances, substantially all of the time spent on the case after the settlement documentation was finalized on September 25, 2018 was unnecessarily and unreasonably incurred, and the Court will not award fees or costs for that time (other than for the 12/18/18 and 1/17/19 time entries, which reflect work in connection with the settlement). The Court declines to award a multiplier, as this case did not present novel or difficult questions of law or fact, the facts were not significantly disputed, and little discovery was required. Plaintiff's counsel expended only slightly more than 20 hours of work on this entire action before accepting Defendants' Section 998 offer and finalizing the settlement agreement. Under the circumstances, the contingent nature of the fee award is far outweighed by the other factors, particularly given Plaintiff's lengthy delay. (See, e.g., Hall v. FCA US LLC (E.D. Cal. May 21, 2018) 2018 WL 2298431 at *8 [declining on similar facts to award multiplier].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: CHAO HONG TAN VS. JUSTIN CHEUNG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Discovery And Production Of Documents, And For Sanctions; Memorandum In Support
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 4, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT JUSTIN CHEUNG's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Discovery And Production Of Documents, And For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Peter Vestal, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The tentative ruling exceeds the system character limits. The Pro Tem Judge will email the parties and/or their counsel the tentative ruling prior to 3 pm. If any party does not receive the tentative ruling by 3 pm, please email the Pro Tem Judge at pvestal@nvlawllp.com and cc: all other parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pvestal@nvlawllp.com with a copy to all other parties by 4 pm stating, without argument, the portion{s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion, and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The moving party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LALLA ELIDRISSI VS. ANGEL TOBIAS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Default; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities And Supporting Declaration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 4, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT ANGEL TOBIAS' Motion To Set Aside Default Hearing required. In light of California's strong policy in favor of deciding cases on their merits, the Court exercises its discretion under Jay v. Mahaffey (2013) 218 Cal.App.4th 1522 to review the evidence submitted with the reply brief and is inclined to grant Defendant's motion to set aside default. Plaintiff shall have an opportunity at the hearing to respond to the evidence submitted with the reply brief and to request additional briefing if necessary. The Court's tentative ruling is as follows: Defendant Angel Tobias' motion to set aside default is granted. Although a proof of service by a registered process server creates a presumption of proper service, the Court credits Ms. Tobias' declaration that she did not reside at the San Francisco address where substitute service was attempted but was leasing a residential property in Oakland. (Tobias Decl. 1; Exh. A.) (See Rogers v. Silverman (1989) 216 Cal.App.3d 1114, 1124 ["Even actual notice is not a substitute for proper service."].) Because service was not effected at Defendant's dwelling house or usual place of abode pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure § 415.20(b), service was improper and the entry of default is vacated pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure 473(d). (See Brown v. Williams (2000) 78 Cal.App.4th 182, 186 fn.4 ["Section 473 empowers a trial court to set aside any judgment or order that is void as a matter of law, for example, . . . because the summons and complaint were not properly served, so that the court lacked personal jurisdiction over a defendant."].) Defendant shall file an answer within 10 days from the entry of this order. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LINCOLN VILLAGE OFFICES LLC VS. ABE TECHNOLOGY INSURANCE BROKERAGE LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Records; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 4, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT SENTINEL INSURANCE COMPANY, LTD'S. Motion To Seal Records; Defendant Sentinel Insurance Company, Ltd.'s motion to seal records is granted. No opposition filed and the motion complies with California Rules of Court, Rules 2.550 and 2.551. The Court finds that there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the portions of the records to be sealed; the overriding interest supports sealing those records; a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BRIAN SCHMITT VS. RUTH A MAGINNIS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Leave To File A First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 4, 2019, Line 8, PLAINTIFFS BRIAN SCHMITT, KRISTA STRYKER'S Motion For Leave To File A First Amended Complaint Plaintiffs Brian Schmitt and Krista Stryker's motion for leave to file a first amended complaint is granted on the condition that Defendant may reopen Plaintiffs' depositions with respect to any new allegations. Plaintiffs have not unduly delayed in presenting their motion. Defendant may challenge the basis for Plaintiffs' newly asserted claim for punitive damages by a motion to strike or motion for summary judgment/adjudication. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CIM URBAN REIT 211 MAIN STREET (SF), LP A ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 4, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS CCSF's motion for judgment on the pleadings as to the fifth, ninth, and tenth causes of action is granted without leave to amend. *The Courts full tenative ruling has been emailed to all counsel.* Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SCOTT BRAUNINGER VS. LISA OH ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 4, 2019, Line 12, DEFENDANTS LISA OH, EDWARD PARK'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT Defendants Lisa Oh and Edward Park's special motion to strike is denied as to the first and fourth causes of action for civil harassment and invasion of privacy and granted as to the second and third causes of action for defamation and false light. *The Courts full tentative ruling has been emailed to all counsel.* Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 4, 2019, Line 13, PLAINTIFF APIC HOTEL METROPOLIS LLC'S Notice Of Application And Hearing For Right To Attach Order And Writ Of Attachment The motion is continued to be heard on June 18, 2019 at 9:30am in Dept. 501. Pursuant to SF Local Rule 8.10A1, that department handles all breach of lease disputes. All courtesy copies should be submitted to Dept. 501. =(302/EPS)
